nativeRange.habitat: Peat swamp forests, blackwater streams, and still or very slow-moving forest pools over leaf litter and organic substrate; highly acidic, tannin-stained, extremely soft water with negligible mineral content.

decorPreferences: dense planting with Java moss, Vesicularia, or similar
floating plants for surface cover and bubble-nest anchoring

diet.notes: Strict micropredator. Requires live or frozen micro-invertebrates: Artemia nauplii (baby brine shrimp), micro-daphnia, Moina, grindal worms, micro-worms, Cyclops, and mosquito larvae. Most specimens refuse dry or prepared foods entirely; the species is highly vulnerable to starvation if live or frozen foods are not provided consistently. Daily or twice-daily feeding of appropriately sized micro-prey is essential.

goodWith: Boraras spp. (micro rasboras) in matched water chemistry
Trigonostigma spp. (harlequin rasboras) only if water parameters overlap
Pangio spp. (kuhli loaches) in matching blackwater conditions
small Otocinclus (with caution, for algae control)
dwarf Corydoras species in matching pH (e.g., C. habrosus)
avoidWith: fast-moving or boisterous fish (competitive feeding pressure)
any species requiring neutral to alkaline water
shrimp species (may be eaten or may harass fry)

imageCandidates.notes: P. deissneri male in display coloration — represents the type species and is the most widely photographed member of the genus in reference sources.
P. nagyi male — commonly traded species; good representative image showing typical genus coloration. Verify current availability.

summary: A genus of 20+ miniature Southeast Asian blackwater gouramis (2.5–4 cm) requiring extreme soft-acid conditions (pH 4.0–5.5, near-zero hardness). Spectacular male breeding coloration; specialist-only due to extreme care demands and active IUCN conservation status across the genus.
careNotes:
GENUS-GROUP ENTRY covering all Parosphromenus species in trade. See also: fw-fish-395 (Licorice Gourami — Parosphromenus deissneri), the type species and original trade introduction; a merge/consolidation review is pending. This entry covers the genus as a whole, encompassing species most frequently encountered in specialist circles: P. deissneri, P. parvulus, P. linkei, P. nagyi, P. harveyi, P. allani, P. ornaticauda, P. filamentosus, and others. All share identical husbandry requirements.

Water chemistry is non-negotiable: Parosphromenus spp. are found exclusively in blackwater peat-swamp environments across Sumatra, Bangka Island, Borneo, and Peninsular Malaysia — some of the most extreme aquatic biotopes in the world. pH must be maintained between 4.0 and 5.5; most species will fail to thrive and often die within weeks in water above pH 6.0. RO or DI water is essential; tap water of virtually any hardness is unsuitable without full reconstitution. Total hardness should be near zero (gH 0–4 dH, kH 0–1). Tannins from Indian Almond (Catappa) leaves, alder cones, peat filtration, or peat extract are strongly recommended and naturally replicate the fish’s habitat.

The tank should be very small (40–60 L is typical for a species or breeding pair), densely planted, with no measurable current — these fish inhabit still pools and stagnant ditches. Floating plants are important for covering bubble nests. Tight-fitting lid is required (labyrinth organ development depends on access to warm, humid air).

Feeding presents the other critical challenge: Parosphromenus are obligate micropredators and the vast majority of specimens will refuse all prepared, pelleted, and freeze-dried foods. Live Artemia nauplii, Moina, micro-daphnia, grindal worms, and micro-worms should be offered daily or twice daily. A steady, reliable supply of live micro-foods is a prerequisite for keeping this genus successfully.

Conservation context: The Parosphromenus Project (parosphromenus-project.org) is an international network of specialist keepers actively breeding and preserving species for conservation, as peat-swamp habitat loss across Sundaland is severe and many species have restricted, rapidly shrinking wild ranges. Several species are assessed as Vulnerable, Endangered, or Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List. Purchasing from reputable breeders involved with conservation breeding is strongly encouraged over wild-caught specimens.
breedingNotes: Bubble-nesters: males construct a small, tight bubble nest typically among floating plants or under a broad leaf near the water surface. Courtship involves intense male coloration display. After spawning, the male guards the bubble nest; eggs and fry should not be disturbed. Incubation is approximately 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming within 3–5 days. The breeding pair should be conditioned extensively with live foods before introduction. A species-only breeding tank (40 L minimum) with heavily acidic, soft water (pH 4.5–5.0) and near-zero flow is standard practice. Fry are tiny and require infusoria, paramecia, or Artemia nauplii rotifers as first foods; micro-daphnia can be introduced once fry are large enough. Rearing is challenging — consistent live micro-food supply and pristine water quality are mandatory. Breeding difficulty is classified as advanced due to strict water chemistry requirements, live food dependency, and fry feeding complexity.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Parosphromenus
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference using P. deissneri as type species. Provided temperature range (24–28°C), pH (4.0–5.0), hardness (0–4 dH), adult size (~3 cm), biotope description, diet (live micro-foods), breeding behaviour (bubble-nester, male guards), and compatibility. Genus-level parameters extrapolated across Parosphromenus spp. following SeriouslyFish genus-treatment convention.

additional.name: FishBase — Parosphromenus deissneri
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3.5 cm TL), temperature (24–27°C), pH (4.0–5.0), native range (Sumatra, Bangka, Peninsular Malaysia), family Osphronemidae, order Anabantiformes. FishBase genus page lists 21 valid species as of last update. Notes extreme blackwater habitat and restricted range.

additional.name: Parosphromenus Project
additional.notes: International specialist conservation breeding network. Provided species inventory (20+ named species), conservation status summaries, husbandry detail confirming pH 4.0–5.5, near-zero hardness, live-food requirements, and importance of captive breeding for threatened species. Primary source for conservation context and genus-group framing.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Liquorice Gourami Care
additional.notes: Trade article covering genus overview; confirmed species in trade (deissneri, nagyi, linkei, parvulus, harveyi), specialist care requirements (pH 4–5.5, extreme soft water, live food dependency), and hobbyist difficulty rating. Corroborated tank sizing (nano species, 40–60 L adequate for pairs), bubble-nest behaviour, and conservation concern framing.

additional.name: FishBase — Parosphromenus genus
additional.notes: Genus-level species list confirming 20+ valid Parosphromenus species; used to establish scope of genus-group entry and enumerate commonly traded species. Cross-referenced with Parosphromenus Project species list.

fish.breedingNotes: Bubble-nester; male builds nest among floating plants and guards eggs/fry. Requires dedicated species tank with pH 4.5–5.0, near-zero hardness, live food conditioning, and minimal disturbance during spawning and fry rearing.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Parosphromenus complex (20+ species) all share extreme blackwater pH 4.0-5.5 requirements. IUCN concerns across the genus. Specialist hobby with active conservation breeding (Parosphromenus Project).
